commentBody: Fun! And a little more challenging than most Sundays
To the creators, congratulations on creating a very challenging Sunday puzzle. For this user, however, it made for quite an unpleasant solving experience.
Dreadful.

These were fun, from two of my faves.
But I’m disappointed that neither Adam nor Simeon would report that building this theme set around WASABI was the way it developed. That was a bit odd!

For half of yesterday’s commenters: here we find out the real origin of the name of that dwarf.  He liked to go everywhere with a large knife, so became known as SNEESY.
Later on the spelling changed.

What did you do?

The help used to take us to a web page. Now it doesn’t. What am I missing?

Switching back and forth is a pain.
Chiasmus, really? Hard to believe I’ve got this far in life never having encountered that word.
Best I could do... https://imgur.com/a/yQ5xKza

My longest streak ever was 622 days. Then on a random Wednesday in September 2024, I solved the puzzle and got... a blue Congratulations message. What!?! No no no no no! I had forgotten to do the Tuesday puzzle. 😫 Well needless to say I resolved to never let that happen again. And today I broke my 622 day streak. Yay! 😊

[In case anyone cares, I never look anything up while solving but once in a blue moon I'll peek here or at the dictionary if I have a typo at the end and am completely stuck]

Thanks for helping me celebrate 🥳
My favorite puzzles are Thursday-Saturday. At best, Sundays are mediocre. Sadly, this one didn't rise to that level.
Definitely an enjoyable challenge. It's nice to see a Sunday stretch its muscle once in a while.

I’m going to be a bit more reactive and circumspect  with my comments today and see what you all think. I will say I enjoyed it and found it quite challenging, both from a theme standpoint and the rest of the clues. Still, it was very obvious these were all computer generated. I’m not at all against that in theory. But I think I would have sacrificed the intricacy of some of these for some friendlier choices. GATECH? CHIAS/CHIASMUS? SOLI? TODDLES? Yes they are all legitimate crossword entries, potentially, just I would have preferred to see something a little more…human, somehow? Also some of the clues, I won’t enumerate them, just seemed a little off in odd ways.
Anyway, I don’t want to be too much of a downer on this, you have my MADRESPECT for creating it and appreciated the unusually high challenge for a Sunday which is refreshing. But now I will sit back and hear what the peanut gallery has to say.
ME HOVER ALL

One of the worst Sundays in recent history. A slog the ENTIRE time. At no point was any fun to be had. 

You can do better, NYT.
This was a poor puzzle. Soli?  Gatech?  My auto correct won’t even let me type these so-called words.  Come on guys.  Two out of three is a nice idea, but poorly done.

Adam and Simeon,
I had many seconds (minutes) of fun with your creation, but it sounds like some folks are having splitting headaches.

Caitlin,
Thanks for the banana split. I'll have it after the meat loaf.

Well, after Saturday’s misery, I’m going to stick my neck out and say i quite liked this one. 

As a Brit i say “pudding” for dessert but i concede that many of my compatriots would say AFTERS. Not my first choice, but I’ll take it. 

What’s more, I don’t know about “words” like GATECH, and I’m not familiar with THE NEA or SHASTA or KSU or ANGI. 

But… i finished it in 33:20! I even guessed ATBATS without a moment’s hesitation! 

So yay!

Overheard at a local health food store, store manager to stockperson: "By Hades! Didn't I tell you to put the bags of hemp hearts on the top shelf, and the chias on the one below?" Now that that's out of the way, what's all this about sonatas being "long"? Here's two examples, from opposite ends of sonatery, each clocking in at under two minutes: Scarlatti: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q969PG1i_4Q&list=RDQ969PG1i_4Q&start_radio=1 Cage: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jRHoKZRYBlY&list=RDjRHoKZRYBlY&start_radio=1 (Both pieces have exactly the same musical form: AABB; that's not a coincidence.) You know what *can* be long? Ragas! Some performances last hours, or all night! There's something not quite right with that clue/entry pair, but I'm not sufficiently versed in Indian music to know exactly what it is. But TIL--and I kid you not--that "raga" can also mean "RED DYE"! https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%B0%E0%A4%BE%E0%A4%97#Sanskrit (will that post as a link, I wonder?)

ASU is the Sundevils. The Wildcats are U of A.
Now I wonder if GATECH was supposed to be UGA.
There was something really unenjoyable about this puzzle’s gimmick. Gatech? For the first time ever, I felt cheated by a NYT crossword.

There is a particular kind of puzzle that I loathe, despise, detest, and really don't like much—one that is peppered with ho hum fills used to support multiple "See ----- across" fills.  I can live with a few, but this one is really over the top.

Since I'm in a vile mood, I might as well pack it in for a while and watch the evening news.

...oops that's what I get for posting before I finish the puzzle.
It was KSU. How can there be two of the same mascot in the same conference? Another sign the world is ending.
loved it loved it loved it. so clever, such fun. thank you.

Very tricky Sunday.  It took me 2:20:32 with no lookups. It was hard work, but hey, considering the appalling state of affairs we are now experiencing, this is exactly the kind of puzzle that helps me transcend the awfulness.  Thank you sir, may I have another?
If Sunday puzzles are supposed to be embiggened Wednesday puzzles, this was far too difficult. This was more like a hard Saturday puzzle.
This was one of the best Sunday puzzles we've had for ages - absolutely loved the challenge and the fact that there was no need to look up any obscure facts.  Thank you Adam and Simeon.
Sorry but I hated it.

Oh, well, I love these two constructors, who excel at entertaining with humor, artistry, skill, and often deviousness.

So, I was not surprised at my marvelous “Hah!” when I saw the theme’s gimmick; nor was I surprised at the fun type of thinking needed to crack the remaining theme answers.

But the pièce de résistance came upon reading the constructors’ notes.  Adam and Simeon were not daunted when the computer spit out 40,000 theme answer possibilities.  Many, I’m guessing, would have tossed the idea entirely at that juncture, or just randomly picked answers that worked.  

But no, they narrowed the list to the cream, then narrowed it further to the cream of the cream. 

That’s a ton of work in the pursuit of excellence.  And a ton more devoted to high-quality cluing and grid design. 

Again, I’m not surprised by it, knowing your work, Adam and Simeon.  Thank you for what you do for Crosslandia, and for a most splendid outing today!
I remembered to check the title and finally got the theme halfway through which helped a lot.  It was a challenging slog but worth it for “Manufacturer’s gross product?”:  SMOG.
A sloppy puzzle filled with lazy clues.  Letters missing from "---ightl-" was quite the nadir.
Not my cup of tea.
Yuck yuck yuck.

Sorry, but "slog" describes this one.
Capped off by spelling CASBAH with a K ???
I don’t understand the hate. This was a lot of fun.
KSU is not in the Big 12 Conference. That clue is wrong.

I solved it but in my opinion it was a bit of a slog. I wondered if I missed something with the theme, were the three words/phrases somehow related? Honestly I would have preferred a theme less Sunday. I have never attempted to construct a crossword puzzle so I have respect for the creators, nevertheless this fell flat for me.

This has been a weekend of some quite difficult puzzles.

I have loved it!  Some other solvers, not so much.

My mother, of blessed memory, who was but a high school graduate, only got the NYT on Sundays, and so did only the Sunday puzzle.  I'd find the partially completed puzzle sitting on the hamper in the bathroom throughout most of the early week. (I guess she solved when no one was bothering her.)  I thought it quite impressive that she did the NYT puzzle at all, so I found it not A BIT ODD that she worked on it in bits and pieces.

I took up the habit right out of college, and not to toot my horn too much, but I've become a much better solver than she ever was.  

So I offer this suggestion to those who had a hard time on this puzzle and gave up, or who are considering giving up.  Take your time.  Put the puzzle down and walk away.  Literally, take a walk.  Or do some chores.  Or have family time.  Let your mind clear.

Go back to the puzzle later.  You'll be surprised what now will jump out at you.  Repeat as necessary.  Just because there's a new puzzle out tomorrow--one that you can probably polish off in minutes--you don't have to give up on this one.

And so what if you don't finish before Thursday?  Finishing four days late is much more satisfying that giving up by 10 pm Sat. night and moving on.

Surprised at all the negativity. This wasn't my favorite, but it felt like a very typical Sunday: clever theme, lots of questionable fill, and definitely harder than Wednesday difficulty.

It even taught me a new word, "chiasmus". I guess it's true what they say- some days you solve the puzzle, and some days the puzzle solves you. (am I doing it right?)
What a tedious puzzle. I would really l hope that constructing it was as tiresome as solving it. This was just a very bad Sunday puzzle.

Surprised this was negative. I enjoyed this challenge very much. Loved the idea!  

Some very tricky stuff like GSIX, but it’s not a big deal.

I might be in a minority here but I rather liked today's puzzle. Had some garbage fill here and there but most of it was just the right amount of challenging and took me close to an hour to crack, which was a nice change from last Sunday's puzzle where I was done in 20min or so. Had a nice aha moment when I figured out the theme too. Thank you to the editors and constructors for another solid Fri-Sat-Sun trio to close off the weekend.

Whereas some solvers seem to have deemed this puzzle unappetizing, I had quite a different experience. It was a slow solve, but not at all a slog for me. It was as if time were moving gently to give me time, and it was as if the puzzle was being patient with me while I figured it out, not just the themers, but the very rich fill. Yesterday I felt I was driving on open roads, in a sports convertible with the top down, as I tackled the quadrants – not a car in sight, just me and expansive views all around. Today I felt I was driving in fresh snow, also with no traffic, but slowly, cautiously, on meandering roads, in an unknown part of the land. Each puzzle had its own appeal and the unique brush strokes of their constructors. Thank you, Adam and Simeon. The admiration and respect you have for each other comes through in your creation. And we're very lucky to have been gifted with the melding of your individual skills and talents. As for the soundtrack of this puzzle, I leave the SONATAS to be brought to you by the incomparable @Bill from Detroit, while I go with that very obvious one that spells KASBAH with a "C"... https://youtu.be/bJ9r8LMU9bQ?si=p61sLUKBudPp9IjI

Didn’t start the Sunday nor do I think I will for a while. 

After 2 hours. I had to raise the white flag and check puzzle the Saturday. I spent an hour staring at the NW corner.  Even with SCHMEARS and CHURRO and  HAREMS. I could not crack that corner. 

RAZORSEDGE. didn’t help. 

That was brutal. It was just 4 puzzles in one.  I ain’t mad. But you make a puzzle with zero connectors and you clue it like that?  That was Saturday +++. 

Again. I ain’t mad. But I really question the cluing decisions. I’m sad I couldn’t gold star. It would have probably been my best solve.

This was a bit tougher than most of the latest Sunday puzzles for me, but I stuck it out and finally got it done.

Having attended a rival school back when both were in the then Big 8, KSU, located in the Little Apple, was a gimme. Most of the rest wasn't that easy, and it turned into a challenge. Need some of those from time to time.

Thanks, Adam and Simeon.

commentBody:
I dug it.

Enjoyed deforming my mind's rubber sheet in various ways to fill in the gimmicks:  where the big phrases didn't come, wrestling with the self-consistent building-block fills usually sufficed.

And when it didn't there were crosses.

No world building or dimension expanding today.

But sometimes an unwonted workout is refreshing and tough.

And how can you beat the central reveal of TWOOUTOFTHREE?  Which was an unnecessary (especially given the title of the grid) but impressive bit of setting braggadocio.

(Reminded me of how Paganini used to make things tougher (not-tougher cause he'd already practiced it that way) for himself by intentionally breaking a string during a performance.)

TIL CHIASMUS and OPE.  Through pure serendipity.

CHIASMUS was cool for Kennedy.

Boo to OPE, natch.  

When's the last time anyone read that something was OPEd?

But all's fair in love and crosswords.
Looks like this one is generating all the feels in the commenters. For me, it was a great, challenging capstone to a great, challenging weekend of puzzling.
Finished it with teamwork but found it very confusing and not fun. Sorry ...

Wow, tough one. I did not get the trick until I was done, got all the two/threes by crosses.
At the beach, seagulls poop on our heads and steal our French fries.  I think this Siegel did the same.   Never did figure out the theme....
I liked the theme and enjoyed figuring most of them out. Spolied by arcane trivia crossing the theme making solving frustrating. Having obscure term as part of the theme not really fair.
I liked it very much, intriguing, fun and enjoyable!

I liked the theme and I found the fill surprisingly gettable for a Simeon Seigel puzzle - I usually find his creations impossible to solve. Still, I CBA to deal with trivia in those huge, Sunday grids so I mostly just revealed it.

A rare, enjoyable Sunday, for me.
Enjoyed the theme, took me a little longer than usual but I'm gonna chalk that up to solving on my phone at 1:30AM. Took me about 34 minutes to get the Gold Star

I hated Saturday’s puzzle. I hated this one more.

It’s not that I mind a difficult solve or obscure clues. I just want a puzzle with a little more… personality? Cleverness? A theme that does more than split a row into two different words?

I don’t know how long this one took, but my clock says 2:35. Some of that was seconds ticking away while I comforted my dogs from thunderstorm noises. The rest of it was me just willing myself to get through it— the storm, AND the puzzle. 

If this is what AI brings us, I am not impressed.

I found the whole business of taking the three strings and making two successive answers to be a bit too challenging character exercise for my mind of late.

I felt like I was starting out all over again anytime I clicked on anything in those feature lines, trying to remind myself what I had figured out before.

Sometimes the pleasures comes in a few moments of sudden inspiration.  Not this time.  This time the pleasure came from having hacked through to the last square, arriving sweaty and dirty.

It felt jungly to me.  Crowded, claustrophobic, uncomfortable.  Every time I have an inspiration and get a few letters I hope for a panoramic view, but all it does is murky the waters.  

(By the way, having daRk for MURK was definitely no help.)

Finally I cleared out the last little patch, realized that I've been hearing about the GSIX all my life and never really thought about it, that I didn't know we were hosting the summer olympics in 2028.  I bet I spent more time staring at _SA than any American anywhere would have.

I guess I'm just not RAHRAH enough.

After all the ugly battles we've fought with emus over the years, is it any wonder a group of them are known as a MOB?

I can just see one of the emus now, cigarette handing out of his beak, saying "Nice little post you got there...it'd be a shame if we had to hold it up."
I hope I'm not the only one who now has a certain Meatloaf song playing in my head
I’ve done the NYT crossword for many years, and I still don’t understand this one after reading the explanation twice. Not a good feeling.

I really enjoyed this one! It stretched my brain from beginning to end. The best part was that the theme answers were tough to parse. Sometimes theme answers are so obvious that I solve the puzzle too quickly. Not this one. I really had to think about each one. I first got GATE CHANGE, with a glimmer of where to go from there, but then the rest of the row was still puzzling. That held for all the themers, where I went back and forth between solving crosses and parsing the theme.

My app finally started linking to Wordplay within the puzzle app, not the NYT app. Bummer. I am not a fan of that. Maybe there is some good reason for this dubious improvement, but it's not an improvement for the crossword. I know I can open Wordplay by searching in the NYT app, but I don't really want to. It's just a drag when an update makes something worse, because it seldom reverts.

Checked it twice and I've still got something wrong but can't see what. Where should I look?

Otherwise loved this!!
Not sure this puzzle creator and I are living in the same world.  Hmmm.
Too much of a stretch and not at all satisfying when you get it right.

Definitely not in my wheelhouse. Despite knowing a fair amount of the clues: KASBAH, PHOEBE, CASABLANCA were delightful gimmes, the theme just felt clunky and contrived. I got it, but I didn’t ’get’ it. 

The new Wordplay layout is driving me insane. What was wrong with the old one?? Not being able to toggle between crossword, Wordplay column and comments really reduces the enjoyment.

This has been the best weekend run in quite a while.  I was ready for some fun as soon as I saw that that we had an Adam Wagner/Simeon Seigel collaboration.  Two names that always make me perk up.

That lost at sea feeling as I solve, wondering just how it's going to make sense is what I'm here for.  Dutifully filling in what I can, waiting for something to emerge, feeling a little off kilter, then being rewarded as it comes into focus.  A perfect Sunday!

Hated this theme. 
 
No connection between the clue parts. Just sets of words running together. I get it. 
Painful. 

The overall puzzle outside the theme was good enough.

I'd like the editors to think about the pleasure of the solvers rather than the cleverness of the constructors. I can respect the work that went into this while still sadly sharing that this was a slog beginning to end.

Came to the comments to see if anyone else was having the same experience I was - got the theme after a few goes round, but finding the solve to be a bit of a slog overall…

Only to read the column and find myself perturbed. To what extent was this crossword created using AI?

In my opinion, any crossword generated with any significant amount of AI - such as generating the theme - should be labelled as such. 

I come to the Times for a real challenge - to enjoy the creativity of people rather a bit smarter than me and learn new things in the process. If I were so inclined, I’m sure I could use AI to generate my own crosswords - so what do I need to pay for a subscription for?

As much as possible in my life, I choose not to engage with AI generated content. The Times should label AI generated content with transparency, so readers can make informed choices.

That was quite a workout even after figuring out what the theme was all about. I like when I have to go back and forth and all around to finish the grid. Manifestos took me awhile to put together, though "was a bit odd/less often" was my first completed theme.

An interesting correspondence I have noticed today: a correllation between commenter names I don't recognize and negative opinions of the puzzle, the constructors and the construction.  (And conversely, among the names I do recognize, mostly the opposite, with a few who are known to be newer solvers being the exception.)

Newcomers, welcome; your contributions are always good for a look at the perspective of those who haven't said anything before.

But do realize that it's not the puzzle's fault if you don't solve it.  Some are harder than others, and some solvers like the additional challenge, so this puzzle is a special treat for those of us with a decent amount of experience.

Sure, feel free to express your exasperation at your frustrating experience, but please don't blame the puzzle for that.  Don't say the constructor was lazy, when what you really mean is that he did something you'd never seen before, and you didn't know what to do with it.  

Someone pointed out earlier that I sounded unpleasant when replying to a complainer.  Maybe I was, but reading all the blame-placing is quite unpleasant to me.

Think of the pitcher who says, "I can't get Ohtani out.  He's a terrible hitter."

I’m sure most of you who regularly solve NYT crosswords have your own habits and definitions of success.  For me, I have found satisfaction in solving nearly all Sundays through Thursdays without any look-ups and thereby enjoy the five-day “streaks” over many weeks, not caring a whit that I am skipping the Friday and Saturday puzzles.

Although the revealer clue in this Sunday puzzle lit up whichever corner of my brain that was engaged to solve five out of the six “split second” clues, the first part of the clue at 87 across, referring to rhetorical devices was my undoing because I had never heard of the answer before.  It did not help that I couldn’t get any of the downs that crossed its first five letters, nor could I get hardly any of the acrosses that crossed those downs. Interestingly, as soon as I looked up a list of 31 rhetorical devices — and I must say I had never heard of most of them — it was easy to pick out the answer and that quickly led to my solving all the others that were stumping me.  One might say that though my brain was working hard, it was hardly working.

My only comment/question is that I thought there was a rule of “etiquette” for commenters to avoid mentioning the answers to any of the clues.  Seeing so many “regulars” here offering some of today’s answers, I guess my understanding was incorrect.

As someone who finds a way to love every puzzle, no matter the path to completion: 

This may've been the least amount of enjoyment I've derived from a Sunday puzzle in forever. 

If this was anywhere close to a daily experience, I'd have never caught the greatest bug of them all....

In a word: LAME.......
This wasn’t my favorite, but I’m certain it will be for someone. I haven’t worked this hard on a Sunday puzzle for a long time. Kudos to the creators.

This was a gem of a Sunday.  The themers were all cleverly constructed and the other fill had some nice cluing. I didn’t catch on to the pattern of the themed clues until I was about three quarters of the way through the puzzle, and then I broke into a big smile and raced to the finish line.  Many thanks to Adam and Simeon for brightening my Sunday morning.

An honest question about the use of AI to produce grids:

Does anyone use AI to produce the clues?

Or is it just the fill?
Really cool Sunday solve. Don’t think I’ve seen a puzzle that combined a triple, double and single solution in one chain like that before. Outstanding construction work!
I had a really good time struggling through this puzzle!  Challenging but not impossible!  Thank you!

Slowest Sunday in months -- got stuck in the block between GIFS and WASABI.

Never heard of chiasmus, but hey ... we solve to learn, we learn to solve.

Sorry.

Super pleased I finished this because I was so close to throwing in the towel. Took me nearly two hours, but now I feel 1) rather brilliant, and 2) that I've staved off any potential dementia for at least a month or two.

As a golfer who had the C and A, I confidently put in CADDYSHACK.
Us hackers quote that movie much much more than Casablana!

Fun on the whole, great concept, and not especially difficult, but some of the split solutions are questionable at best. Technically correct words and phrases, yes, but ones you could probably go through a decent lifetime without hearing...

Side note: AFTERS isn't very commonly used here outside of old people and/or a few northern towns - for the majority it refers to the postscript to a heavy night of revelry. Not the first arcane British regionalism/generationism, in fairness. PUDDING is far more widespread. Might have been better to have eschewed the 'wisdom' of ChatGPT on that one!

Steve L. wrote: "...this puzzle is a special treat for those of us with a decent amount of experience." Well, I have more than a decent amount of experience, and this was no treat for me. 

I winced when I saw one of the two names of the constructors, who happens to be one of my least favorite, and I was not disabused by that opinion. Just like I light up with joy when I see someone like Robyn Weintraub's name on a puzzle, I cringe when I see the one today.

I walked away several times, came back and filled in a few more answers. In the end, I finished it without help, but had no sense of accomplishment, just a big sigh that it was finally over. I didn't find the clues or the theme clever, and the theme was just annoying. Many Sundays make me laugh when I fill in theme answers. All I got from this was, okay, another one filled in.

Random thought:  

Back when presidents were more clever -- or clever at all -- one of them said, and to paraphrase, We do these things not because they are easy, but because they are hard.

I liked it a lot. I can’t say I loved it, because the northeast corner was a bear. But I respected the theme, the fill, and the challenge. Overall great to require more thinking than usual on a Sunday. I usually fill in along on first passes, and today took several.
Might be the first Sunday puzzle where the “aha” moment when the theme is realized, never occurred for me. “Congratulations” tone went off and I was still clueless.

Highlight for me was when I saw "5D Deal with it!" and immediately entered SHOE. 

Needless to say, that (misguided) thrill didn't last long, and my solving experience quickly turned into a lengthy slog. Filled the puzzle correctly without assistance, but took about 52 minutes. 

Used the theme extensively to help me solve this, but simply didn't enjoy this one. To me, this puzzle felt more like a technical exercise in puzzle construction than an engaging start to my Sunday.

Hope others felt it warranted MADRESPECT.

Weekends of hard puzzles like this are what made the NYTXW. That’s what brought us all here, and will keep us coming back here, wanting to enjoy it or needing to complain about it. So to those who (unlike me) disliked today’s excellent puzzle, the theme is clearly directed:

I want you.
I neeeed you.
But there ain’t no way I’m ever gonna love you.

I’d say I done did this puppy
in sixteen minutes flat, but


I can't lie, I can't tell you that I'm somethin' I'm not
No matter how I try, 
I'll never be able
To give you somethin', 
somethin' that I just haven't got.

Yikes! This was a tough one for me.

Got started at a good clip, skipping, as usual, the themers (especially in the [See x-Across] format), and then I found myself getting stuck.

I probably spent the entire twenty minutes between my average and today's time after I had the grid filled in, searching for the error.

Never heard of PANANG, so I thought GATECHArGE was a reasonable crossing. Similarly, I have no idea what schools are in the Big 12 (hint: a lot more than twelve), or who plays drums for Green Day, so I figured cSU and SOCcEr were possibly right.

I've spent many hours riding the ATRAIN, so that was a gimme, especially clued as it was. TWOOUTOFTHREE [ain't bad], was also pretty obvious, but I couldn't find a video link of Vincent Price introducing the singer on SNL, "Ladies and gentlemen, meet . . .Loaf".

Thankks!
Fun puzzle once I figured out the “split seconds”. I used help on two or three answers, but none effected the across solutions.

Whew. Pretty amazing piece of construction. Took me quite a while to tumble to the trick and even then it was a good long workout. And... didn't quite finish it successfully. Because there appears to be a lot of hate for this one, I'd like to explain a little of why I loved it, knowing YMMV.

First, I look for something very chewy, a mental feast that requires sustained thought before things become clear. The last three days were feasts in that respect. Like other feasts, I don't need them every weekend, but I hope we have these often enough that folks aren't surprised by them any more. These are back to pre-pandemic difficulty. I confess I had gotten out of the habit of not being able to solve every puzzle. 

On the other hand, when this one became clear, it was really, really clear, with three possible ways to check each themer. So when there was a new word, CHIASMUS, there were two other ways to check it: the crosses and MUSCLEMEN. And what a wonderful new word to learn today!

On another subject, I do wish people wouldn't comment specifically to contradict the subjective emotional experiences of others. When a person comes here and complains about a puzzle, it seems pretty clear to me that they are blowing off steam and possibly seeking commiseration from others with the same frustrating experience. That's human, and even if I enjoyed the same puzzle myself, I get why they do it.

Likewise, I understand why people comment to say that they found a crossword to be delightful or pleasantly challenging or cleverly constructed. But I have noticed that the people who dislike a puzzle are much less likely to tell the people who liked it that they are wrong then the people who enjoyed it are to tell those who didn't that they are inept or inexperienced or simply possessed of bad taste.

My rule of thumb is to comment to agree, or to simply say nothing. I am weary of people telling others that their opinions are somehow incorrect.

commentBody:
@John

@Matt

In addition to what John and Steve L said: if you look at xwstats times for a Sunday puzzle on Saturday night or early Sunday AM, the “median” solve time is basically Steve L’s time :)

That same figure is up to nearly 33 minutes as of 9:45 AM ET, and it will continue creeping up over the rest of the day.

Also, the difficulty characterization is strictly a function of a predefined formula, based on how slow/fast the population’s solve times were, relative to their personal averages for the relevant day of week, computed over a 10-week rolling window (roughly speaking).

As the puzzles have gotten easier on average over the last few years, the xwstats population’s average solve times have gone down considerably, and so the xwstats “difficulty” classifications have become increasingly sensitive to relatively small deviations from the population’s average, as expressed in absolute units.

But no, at the moment, AI can certainly generate a grid and clue it—maybe even come up with a rudimentary theme—but I don’t think anything close to even the worst or easiest puzzles published here. Again, if there are any gurus here who have examples to prove me wrong I’d love to see.

@Matt
I can think of no other method by which CHIASMUS would appear in a word list. I certainly wouldn't be in mine. I don't use AI for anything, as I fear it would alter my writing style beyond recognition. And I don't write much.

@Matt
An issue with today's LLMs is that they are predictive. Given a starting phrase and a context, what would be the most likely next word or phrase? The result is invariably a blanding out, a loss of the eccentric, the oddball.

Some recent research has shown that gravitating toward the most common answer like this also affects users. One study showed steadily decreasing inventiveness when undergrads were engaged in a design task with the help of an LLM compared with another set of students who designed without that. And this loss of creativity continued in the week following the experiment. Another study showed that personas generated by an LLM, though varied, did not capture the kinds of eccentricity you could find if you looked for the oddest responses in, say, a large survey of real humans. The market researchers in that study said they needed the variation you get from real people rather than what the LLM generated.

There are useful ways to use LLMs, which is a subject of my research. But oddball thinking turns out to be very useful in puzzle creation.
